name: Model Inversion Attack
description: Attacker with query access to ML model can reconstruct training data through repeated inference queries, extracting sensitive information encoded in model weights.
mitigations: Implement differential privacy during training
Add noise to model outputs
Limit query rate and complexity
Monitor for suspicious query patterns

name: Adversarial Example Attack
description: Malicious actor can craft imperceptible perturbations to input data causing the model to produce incorrect predictions, bypassing security controls.
mitigations: Implement adversarial training with robust examples
Use input sanitization and validation
Deploy ensemble models for consensus
Monitor prediction confidence scores

name: Training Data Poisoning
description: Attacker with access to training data pipeline can inject malicious examples causing the model to learn incorrect patterns or backdoors.
mitigations: Implement data provenance tracking
Use anomaly detection on training data
Apply data validation and sanitization
Maintain secure data pipelines with access controls

name: Model Extraction via API
description: Attacker querying ML API can extract a functionally equivalent model through systematic probing, stealing intellectual property and enabling further attacks.
mitigations: Implement query rate limiting
Add prediction confidence thresholds
Monitor for systematic probing patterns
Use model watermarking techniques

name: Prompt Injection in LLM
description: User providing malicious prompts can manipulate LLM behavior to bypass safety controls, leak system instructions, or generate harmful content.
mitigations: Implement prompt filtering and validation
Use system message hardening
Apply output content filtering
Maintain separate contexts for system and user content

name: Training-Serving Skew
description: Differences between training and production environments can cause model performance degradation or unexpected behavior, leading to incorrect predictions.
mitigations: Maintain consistent feature engineering pipelines
Implement continuous validation in production
Use feature store for consistency
Monitor for data distribution drift

name: Model Backdoor
description: Malicious insider during model development can embed hidden triggers causing model to misbehave on specific inputs while performing normally otherwise.
mitigations: Implement model verification and testing
Use trusted training environments
Apply code review for model training code
Test model behavior on edge cases

name: Membership Inference Attack
description: Attacker can determine if specific data points were in the training set by analyzing model predictions, violating privacy of training data subjects.
mitigations: Use differential privacy during training
Implement prediction confidence capping
Apply regularization techniques
Limit access to model confidence scores

name: Feature Inference Attack
description: Attacker with model access can infer sensitive features about individuals that were not directly predicted but correlate with model outputs.
mitigations: Implement feature suppression for sensitive attributes
Use fairness-aware training techniques
Apply output perturbation
Conduct privacy impact assessments

name: Model Supply Chain Attack
description: Attacker compromising pre-trained models or ML libraries can inject malicious code affecting all downstream applications using these components.
mitigations: Verify checksums of downloaded models
Use trusted model repositories only
Scan models for embedded malware
Maintain software bill of materials (SBOM)

name: Inference Cost Attack
description: Attacker sending computationally expensive inference requests can exhaust resources and incur high costs, causing denial of service or financial damage.
mitigations: Implement request size limits
Use cost-based rate limiting
Deploy inference request queuing
Monitor and alert on cost anomalies

name: Model Bias Exploitation
description: Attacker discovering model biases can exploit them to receive favorable predictions or cause discriminatory outcomes for specific groups.
mitigations: Conduct bias audits across demographics
Implement fairness metrics monitoring
Use bias mitigation techniques during training
Maintain diverse training datasets

name: Gradient Leakage in Federated Learning
description: Malicious participant in federated learning can extract private training data from shared gradients, compromising data privacy of other participants.
mitigations: Implement secure aggregation protocols
Use differential privacy on gradients
Apply gradient clipping and noise
Verify participant identities

name: Reward Hacking in RL
description: RL agent can discover unintended ways to maximize reward function that violate intended objectives, causing harmful or unexpected behaviors.
mitigations: Design robust reward functions with constraints
Implement human-in-the-loop oversight
Use reward shaping techniques
Monitor agent behavior for anomalies

name: Model Drift Detection Bypass
description: Attacker can gradually shift input distribution to evade drift detection while degrading model performance over time.
mitigations: Implement multiple drift detection metrics
Use statistical tests with tight thresholds
Monitor feature correlations over time
Maintain human review of edge cases

name: Explainability Manipulation
description: Attacker can craft inputs that produce misleading explanations while causing incorrect predictions, undermining trust in model interpretability.
mitigations: Use multiple explanation methods
Validate explanations against ground truth
Implement explanation consistency checks
Apply adversarial robustness to explainers

name: Transfer Learning Attack
description: Attacker providing malicious pre-trained model can embed vulnerabilities that persist through fine-tuning, affecting downstream tasks.
mitigations: Verify source of pre-trained models
Test models on adversarial examples
Apply safety fine-tuning
Use model cards and documentation

name: AutoML Manipulation
description: Attacker with access to AutoML pipeline can manipulate hyperparameter search space to cause poor model selection or embed vulnerabilities.
mitigations: Implement constraints on search space
Validate selected models thoroughly
Use trusted AutoML frameworks
Monitor AutoML experiment metrics

name: Privacy Budget Exhaustion
description: Attacker making repeated queries can exhaust differential privacy budget, allowing reconstruction of sensitive training data.
mitigations: Implement strict privacy budget tracking
Use query result caching
Apply per-user privacy budgets
Monitor and alert on budget depletion

name: Model Denial via Input Complexity
description: Attacker crafting maximally complex inputs can cause model inference to consume excessive time and memory, creating denial of service.
mitigations: Implement input complexity limits
Use timeout mechanisms for inference
Apply input preprocessing and validation
Deploy resource monitoring and throttling

name: Training Data Exfiltration
description: Attacker with access to ML infrastructure can exfiltrate training datasets containing sensitive personal data, intellectual property, or proprietary business information.
mitigations: Encrypt training data at rest and in transit
Implement strict access controls with audit logging
Use data loss prevention monitoring on storage egress
Apply data minimization to retain only required features

name: Feature Store Poisoning
description: Attacker with write access to a shared feature store can corrupt feature values, silently degrading model accuracy across all models consuming those features.
mitigations: Implement feature validation and schema enforcement
Use version control and immutable snapshots for features
Monitor feature distributions for unexpected changes
Require approval workflows for feature store writes

name: Data Label Manipulation
description: Malicious annotator or compromised labeling pipeline can systematically mislabel training examples, causing the model to learn incorrect decision boundaries.
mitigations: Implement multi-annotator consensus with disagreement detection
Use automated label quality checks and outlier detection
Audit annotator accuracy with known ground-truth samples
Maintain provenance tracking for all label changes

name: Indirect Prompt Injection
description: Attacker embedding malicious instructions in external data sources (documents, websites, emails) processed by LLM can hijack model behavior when the LLM retrieves and processes that content.
mitigations: Sanitize all retrieved content before including in prompts
Implement content provenance verification
Use separate context windows for system and retrieved content
Apply output validation against expected behavior patterns

name: LLM Jailbreaking via Role-Play
description: Attacker using creative role-play scenarios, multi-turn manipulation, or encoding tricks can bypass LLM safety guardrails to extract harmful content or system instructions.
mitigations: Implement multi-layer content filtering on inputs and outputs
Use classifier-based guardrails independent of the LLM itself
Monitor conversations for known jailbreak patterns
Regularly red-team and update safety fine-tuning

name: Context Window Manipulation
description: Attacker can craft inputs that fill or manipulate the context window to push system instructions out of context, causing the LLM to ignore safety directives and constraints.
mitigations: Reserve fixed context budget for system instructions
Implement input length limits with graceful truncation
Use structured prompting that anchors system directives
Monitor for abnormally long or padded inputs

name: ML Pipeline Code Injection
description: Attacker with access to ML pipeline configuration can inject malicious code into training scripts, data preprocessing steps, or model evaluation hooks, executing arbitrary code in the training environment.
mitigations: Implement code review for all pipeline changes
Use containerized and sandboxed training environments
Sign and verify all pipeline artifacts
Restrict write access to pipeline definitions

name: Model Registry Tampering
description: Attacker with access to the model registry can replace production models with compromised versions or modify model metadata to redirect inference to malicious models.
mitigations: Implement model signing and integrity verification
Use immutable model versions with content-based addressing
Require multi-party approval for production model promotions
Audit all model registry operations

name: Experiment Tracking Manipulation
description: Attacker modifying experiment tracking records can falsify model performance metrics, leading to promotion of underperforming or compromised models to production.
mitigations: Use append-only experiment logs with tamper detection
Implement independent model validation before promotion
Require reproducible training runs with deterministic seeds
Cross-validate metrics against held-out validation sets

name: Dataset Supply Chain Compromise
description: Attacker compromising public dataset repositories or data providers can inject poisoned data that propagates to all downstream models trained on those datasets.
mitigations: Verify dataset checksums and signatures before use
Use trusted and curated dataset sources
Implement automated data quality scans on ingestion
Maintain a dataset bill of materials with provenance

name: ML Framework Vulnerability Exploitation
description: Attacker exploiting vulnerabilities in ML frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn) can achieve remote code execution or data exfiltration through malicious model files or crafted inputs.
mitigations: Keep ML frameworks updated with security patches
Use safe model serialization formats (avoid pickle)
Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities
Run model loading in sandboxed environments

name: GPU Side-Channel Attack
description: Co-tenant on shared GPU infrastructure can exploit side-channel leakage to extract model parameters, training data patterns, or inference inputs from adjacent workloads.
mitigations: Use dedicated GPU instances for sensitive workloads
Implement memory clearing between tenant workloads
Monitor GPU utilization patterns for anomalies
Use confidential computing with GPU memory encryption

name: Agentic Tool Abuse
description: AI agent with tool-calling capabilities can be manipulated through prompt injection to misuse its tools, executing unintended database queries, API calls, or file operations.
mitigations: Implement tool-level authorization and scope restrictions
Require human approval for destructive or sensitive tool calls
Use allowlists for tool parameters and target resources
Log and audit all tool invocations with full context

name: Model Confidentiality Breach via Distillation
description: Attacker with API access can use knowledge distillation techniques to train a student model that replicates the proprietary model's behavior at a fraction of the cost.
mitigations: Implement output perturbation to degrade distillation quality
Monitor for high-volume systematic query patterns
Use rate limiting with progressive throttling
Deploy model watermarking for theft detection

name: Discriminatory Output Generation
description: ML model can produce systematically biased outputs that discriminate against protected groups in high-stakes decisions such as hiring, lending, or criminal justice, creating legal and ethical liability.
mitigations: Implement pre-deployment fairness testing across protected attributes
Use counterfactual fairness evaluation on test datasets
Deploy real-time bias monitoring on production predictions
Establish fairness review boards for high-stakes applications

name: Proxy Discrimination via Correlated Features
description: Model can learn to use seemingly neutral features (zip code, browser language, device type) as proxies for protected attributes, producing discriminatory outcomes while appearing unbiased on surface metrics.
mitigations: Conduct proxy feature analysis to identify correlated attributes
Use causal inference methods to detect indirect discrimination
Implement feature ablation studies for fairness impact
Apply adversarial debiasing to remove proxy signal from representations

name: Demographic Parity Violation
description: Model producing significantly different positive prediction rates across demographic groups can violate regulatory requirements and cause disparate impact, even without explicit use of protected attributes.
mitigations: Monitor demographic parity, equalized odds, and calibration metrics
Implement threshold adjustment per group where legally permissible
Use in-processing fairness constraints during training
Conduct regular disparate impact analysis with documented thresholds

name: Alert Fatigue Exploitation
description: Attacker can deliberately trigger high volumes of low-severity alerts to desensitize operators, then execute the actual attack while monitoring teams are overwhelmed or ignoring notifications.
mitigations: Implement alert prioritization with severity-based routing
Use alert correlation to group related events and reduce noise
Set dynamic alert thresholds that adapt to baseline activity
Maintain separate high-priority channels for critical security alerts

name: Monitoring Blind Spot Exploitation
description: Attacker can identify gaps in monitoring coverage — unmonitored model endpoints, unchecked input distributions, or missing output validation — and exploit these blind spots to degrade or manipulate model behavior undetected.
mitigations: Conduct monitoring coverage audits against all model inputs and outputs
Implement canary inputs with known expected outputs to verify monitoring
Deploy shadow scoring to detect discrepancies in unmonitored paths
Use chaos engineering to validate alerting for known failure modes

name: ML Audit Log Tampering
description: Attacker with access to ML infrastructure can modify or delete training logs, experiment records, and inference audit trails to hide evidence of model tampering or data poisoning.
mitigations: Use append-only, immutable storage for all ML audit logs
Implement cryptographic chaining of log entries for tamper detection
Replicate audit logs to independent write-once storage
Restrict log deletion permissions with mandatory approval workflows

name: PII Leakage via Embeddings
description: Embedding vectors generated from text containing personal data can encode recoverable PII, allowing attackers with access to vector databases or embedding APIs to reconstruct names, addresses, or other sensitive information.
mitigations: Scrub PII from text before embedding generation
Apply dimensionality reduction to degrade PII recoverability
Use differential privacy during embedding model training
Implement access controls on embedding stores equal to source data classification

name: Differential Privacy Bypass via Auxiliary Data
description: Attacker combining differentially private model outputs with external auxiliary datasets can re-identify individuals or infer sensitive attributes that the privacy mechanism was designed to protect.
mitigations: Account for auxiliary data in privacy budget calculations
Use composition theorems to track cumulative privacy loss
Implement group-level differential privacy for correlated records
Conduct linkage attack simulations with publicly available datasets

name: Data Lineage Corruption
description: Attacker modifying data lineage metadata can obscure the provenance of training data, hiding poisoned sources and making it impossible to trace model behavior back to its data origins.
mitigations: Use cryptographically signed lineage records at each processing step
Implement immutable data lineage storage with append-only writes
Cross-validate lineage metadata against actual data checksums
Require lineage integrity verification before model training

name: Synthetic Data Re-identification
description: Synthetic datasets generated from real data can retain enough statistical patterns to allow re-identification of individuals when combined with auxiliary information, undermining the privacy guarantees of synthetic data.
mitigations: Evaluate synthetic data with membership inference and linkage attack tests
Apply differential privacy guarantees during synthetic data generation
Limit the number of synthetic records that closely match rare real records
Use privacy metrics (k-anonymity, l-diversity) to validate synthetic outputs

name: Byzantine Model Aggregation Attack
description: Malicious participants in distributed or federated training can submit corrupted model updates that poison the aggregated global model while evading simple averaging-based detection.
mitigations: Use Byzantine-fault-tolerant aggregation algorithms (Krum, trimmed mean)
Implement anomaly detection on submitted model updates
Require reputation scoring and trust levels for participants
Cross-validate aggregated model against held-out validation data each round

name: Model Shard Poisoning in Distributed Training
description: Attacker compromising a single node in distributed training can poison its local model shard, introducing subtle backdoors that propagate during parameter synchronization across the cluster.
mitigations: Implement integrity verification on parameter updates between nodes
Use redundant training with consensus validation across shards
Monitor gradient statistics per node for distributional anomalies
Isolate training nodes with hardware-level attestation
